Question: When making the 5-hour drive home from visiting her parents, Lisa ran into bad weather. She was able to drive 176 miles while the weather was good, but then driving 10 mph slower, went 81 miles in the bad weather. How fast did she drive when the weather was bad?
o3 Answer:
Let  

v = speed while the weather was still good (mph)  
v – 10 = speed after the weather turned bad (mph)

Total driving time = 5 hours.

Time = distance ÷ speed, so

176 miles / v  +  81 miles / (v – 10)  = 5.

Clear denominators by multiplying by v(v – 10):

176(v – 10) + 81v = 5v(v – 10)

176v – 1 760 + 81v = 5v² – 50v  
257v – 1 760 = 5v² – 50v  
0 = 5v² – 307v + 1 760.

Solve the quadratic:

v = [307 ± √(307² – 4·5·1 760)] / (2·5)  
v = [307 ± √(94 249 – 35 200)] / 10  
v = [307 ± √59 049] / 10  
v = [307 ± 243] / 10.

Possible values:  

v = (307 + 243)/10 = 55   or  v = (307 – 243)/10 = 6.4.

A speed of 6.4 mph is impossible for highway driving, so v = 55 mph.

Therefore, speed in bad weather:

v – 10 = 55 mph – 10 mph = 45 mph.

Lisa drove 45 mph while the weather was bad.
